titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Samuel Smith Wood Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Staff of Manuscripts and Archives

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

conventiondeclaration.abbr: Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual
conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Saturday October 24, 2020 at 08:37

did.abstract: The papers consist of correspondence relating to the life of the Samuel Smith Wood family in Greytown, Nicaragua, the bombardment of the city by the American ship, Cyane, in July, 1854, and claims rising out of that incident. A particularly long account is given in a letter (July 15, 1854) by Wood's son, Alexander McCready Wood. Also in the papers are letters (1849) by Samuel S. Wood to his wife describing a trip through the gold regions of San Juan and San Francisco, California and a family genealogy.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: Entire collection also available on microfilm (788 frames on 1 reel, 35mm.) from Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library, at cost. Order no. HM164.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The entire collection is available on microfilm. Patrons must use HM 164 instead of the originals.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Samuel Smith Wood Papers (MS 1083). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Samuel Wood Papers date 1849-1920 and are principally concerned with the Wood family of Greytown, Nicaragua, and the bombardment of Greytown by the American ship Cyane in 1854.

The rush across Central America by Forty-Niners bound for California resulted in a backwash of Americans who remained there to engage in trade. Those gathered at San Juan del Norte, or Greytown, organized themselves into an independent free-trade city. Among the prominent merchants of this place was Samuel Smith Wood who carried on an extensive and lucrative business. Among the more substantial citizens, Wood possessed a large and valuable stock of merchandise and was the owner of a number of buildings at Greytown.

In 1854, a dispute broke out between some of the residents of Greytown and the American minister there, and the United States government dispatched the ship Cyane to investigate. An account of what followed is given in a lengthly letter written by Alexander McCready Wood, son of Samuel Smith Wood, dated 1854 July 15. He writes: "The Cyane arrived here on Tuesday night last and on Wednesday morning Captain Hollis notified the inhabitants of our town that indemnification to the amount of $28,000 was demanded by his government and unless the terms were complied with by eight o'clock on Thursday morning, he would blow and bombard the town to atoms which the inhabitants failing to do, his threat was actually and faithfully carried into effect and our once pleasant little town lies in a heap of smouldering ruins."

Captain Hollis' act, which was generally condemned, gave rise to numerous actions against the American government. The Wood family presented a memorial to Congress asking indemnity for their large losses. Many of the papers are concerned with this case.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Samuel Smith Wood worked in flour manufacturing in New York City until ca. 1826. He moved to a farm in Coutlandville, where he remained until 1834. Wood then went to California prospecting for gold. In 1852 he established a trading post at San Juan del Norte (Greytown), Nicaragua.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection are in the public domain. There are no restrictions on use. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: A copy of this material is available in digital form from Manuscripts and Archives. Contact Manuscripts and Archives at mssa.assist@yale.edu to request access to the digital copy.
